text:
CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST — RESTAGING

CLINICAL INDICATION:
71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), originally diagnosed with high-grade acinar adenocarcinoma (Gleason 9, Grade Group 5), stage IVB disease with bone and lymph node metastases. Known germline BRCA2 mutation. Disease history notable for progression through ADT/abiraterone and subsequent enzalutamide. Most recently treated with olaparib (dose-reduced to 100 mg BID for cumulative myelosuppression), which was discontinued upon confirmation of visceral progression with hepatic metastases. Initiated on carboplatin (AUC 3, dose-reduced from AUC 4 following cycle 1 toxicity) plus cabazitaxel (15 mg/m2, dose-reduced from 20 mg/m2) with filgrastim support. Now presenting for restaging after completion of 2 cycles. PSA at time of scan: 18.6 ng/mL. Prior hospitalization for febrile neutropenia after cycle 1. Evaluate for treatment response versus progression.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed with intravenous iodinated contrast (Omnipaque 350, 100 mL IV, administered via right antecubital access) following a standard oncologic restaging protocol with arterial and portal venous phase acquisitions through the abdomen and pelvis. Delayed axial images were obtained through the upper abdomen for hepatic lesion characterization. Axial, coronal, and sagittal multiplanar reformats generated and reviewed. Lung windows, mediastinal windows, and bone windows reviewed. No oral contrast administered per department protocol.

Comparison: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed approximately 7 weeks prior at time of olaparib restaging (hepatic lesions 1.2 cm and 1.0 cm at that time); PET/CT with FDG from same period reviewed for reference. MRI liver available for additional comparison.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways:
The trachea is midline and patent. The airways are clear to the segmental level bilaterally. There is no endobronchial lesion identified.

The previously identified right lower lobe pulmonary nodules have grown since the comparison study. The dominant right lower lobe nodule, previously measuring 5 mm at initial detection and 9 mm on the comparison CT, now measures approximately 12 mm in greatest dimension (image series 3, image 47). A second right lower lobe nodule, previously 6 mm, now measures 8 mm. A third nodule in the right lower lobe, not definitively identified on the prior CT, measures 5 mm and is considered new. The nodules are solid, non-calcified, and without cavitation or surrounding ground-glass change. Their morphology, location, and pattern of interval growth in the setting of known widely metastatic prostate carcinoma is highly concerning for pulmonary metastatic disease. No left lower lobe nodules. No left upper or right upper lobe nodules. No perifissural or subpleural nodules with clearly benign morphology elsewhere.

No pleural effusions bilaterally. No pneumothorax.

Mediastinum and Hila:
The cardiac silhouette is within normal limits for size. There is no pericardial effusion. The aorta is atherosclerotic in appearance without evidence of dissection or significant aneurysmal change. Mediastinal contour is unremarkable. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes by size criteria. Hilar structures are symmetric; no hilar adenopathy. The superior vena cava and central pulmonary vasculature appear patent.

Chest Wall and Soft Tissues:
No chest wall mass or subcutaneous nodule. Breast tissue symmetric (gynecomastia is noted bilaterally, mild, consistent with known long-term androgen deprivation therapy).

Osseous Structures (Chest):
Multiple foci of abnormal bony density are identified throughout the visualized skeleton, consistent with the extensive osseous metastatic disease previously documented on bone scintigraphy. Within the thoracic skeleton, there are sclerotic and mixed lytic-sclerotic lesions in multiple ribs bilaterally (consistent with known previously identified rib lesions). A sclerotic lesion in the T9 vertebral body is again seen. There is no pathologic fracture of the visible thoracic vertebral bodies or ribs. No soft tissue component extending into the spinal canal is identified on this examination, though MRI would provide superior evaluation if clinically indicated. The shoulders are partially imaged; a previously documented lesion in the right proximal humerus is not well-characterized on this chest portion of the study but is noted in the shoulder girdle region.

---

ABDOMEN:

Liver:
The liver is normal in size and morphology. The hepatic contour is smooth. No biliary ductal dilation centrally or peripherally. Intrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated. The gallbladder is surgically absent (history not provided but no gallbladder identified; incidental note).

Importantly, the two hepatic lesions previously characterized on MRI and FDG PET/CT have grown since the reference comparison study:
— Right hepatic lobe, segment VI-VII junction: A hypodense lesion on portal venous phase imaging measuring approximately 1.6 cm in greatest axial dimension (previously 1.2 cm on MRI; 1.1 cm on the CT performed at time of initial hepatic detection). This lesion demonstrates peripheral rim enhancement on arterial phase with partial washout on delayed imaging, morphologically consistent with a hypervascular metastatic deposit.
— Right hepatic lobe, segment VI: A second hypodense lesion measuring approximately 1.4 cm (previously 1.0 cm on MRI; 0.9 cm on initial CT detection).
— Right hepatic lobe, segment V: A new, third hypodense lesion measuring approximately 7 mm, previously described as 6 mm at the last imaging timepoint and felt to represent early new disease. On the current examination this lesion demonstrates similar enhancement characteristics to the dominant lesions and has grown modestly, further supporting metastatic etiology.
— Additionally, a fourth small hepatic hypodensity measuring approximately 5 mm is identified in segment IV, which was not appreciated on prior imaging and may represent an additional new metastatic deposit, though its small size and potential partial volume averaging limits characterization. Follow-up characterization is warranted.

The main portal vein, right portal vein, and left portal vein are patent. The hepatic veins are patent. No thrombosis identified.

Spleen:
Mildly enlarged spleen measuring approximately 13.2 cm in craniocaudal dimension (normal upper limit ~13 cm). This is a mild, nonspecific finding in the context of chronic anemia and systemic disease burden; no focal splenic lesion identified.

Pancreas:
The pancreas is normal in size and parenchymal attenuation. No ductal dilation. No focal pancreatic mass or cyst. Periampullary region unremarkable.

Adrenal Glands:
Bilateral adrenal glands are within normal limits. No adrenal mass or nodule. No hemorrhage.

Kidneys and Collecting System:
The kidneys are symmetric in size and morphology. No hydronephrosis. A few small bilateral cortical cysts are identified (largest right kidney 1.4 cm, stable), consistent with benign simple cysts. No solid renal mass. No nephrolithiasis. Note is made of mildly increased cortical echogenicity (decreased corticomedullary differentiation on CT), consistent with the known history of chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine 1.6–1.8 mg/dL).

Bowel:
Small bowel loops are normal in caliber and distribution without evidence of obstruction or wall thickening. The colon contains stool throughout without distension. No free air. Appendix not definitively visualized but no periappendiceal fat stranding.

Ascites/Peritoneum:
No ascites. No peritoneal implants or omental caking identified.

Abdominal Aorta and Vasculature:
The abdominal aorta is mildly ectatic in the infrarenal segment (approximately 2.4 cm in diameter), stable. Mural calcification is present. No dissection. The celiac, superior mesenteric, and inferior mesenteric arteries are patent at their origins.

Abdominal Lymph Nodes:
Retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy is again demonstrated and has worsened since the prior examination:
— Right para-aortic lymph node: 1.7 cm in short axis (previously 1.4 cm). This node has grown and is now more conspicuous, consistent with progressive nodal disease.
— Aortocaval node: 1.3 cm in short axis (previously 1.1 cm), mildly enlarged and progressing.
— Left para-aortic nodal chain: A cluster of nodes measuring up to 1.0 cm (previously borderline/subcentimeter), now approaching pathological threshold.
No hepatic hilar adenopathy.

---

PELVIS:

Prostate and Seminal Vesicles:
The prostate gland and seminal vesicle region demonstrate post-treatment changes, with no significant change from prior examinations. No discrete pelvic fluid collection.

Bladder:
The urinary bladder is well distended with no wall thickening, filling defect, or mucosal irregularity.

Rectum and Sigmoid:
No rectal or sigmoid wall thickening or mass.

Pelvic Lymph Nodes:
Pelvic lymphadenopathy is progressing:
— Right obturator lymph node: 2.7 cm in short axis (previously 2.4 cm at last restaging CT). This node has grown further and is now one of the dominant sites of nodal disease in the pelvis.
— Right external iliac lymph node: 1.5 cm (stable to slightly increased from prior).
— Left external iliac lymph node: 1.1 cm (stable).
— Left common iliac lymph node: 1.4 cm (stable).

Osseous Structures (Pelvis and Lumbar Spine):
Extensive osseous metastatic disease is again demonstrated. Sclerotic lesions are identified in the left iliac crest (two foci previously noted), the right acetabulum (previously documented), the sacrum (previously noted on bone scintigraphy), the left femoral head region, and the right femoral neck region (status post palliative radiation therapy; sclerotic changes are again noted, no acute cortical disruption on CT). The L2 and L4 vertebral bodies demonstrate sclerotic foci. No acute pathologic fracture is identified in the visible axial skeleton. No epidural soft tissue extension is identified on this modality. There is no evidence of impending pathologic fracture at the hip (cortical integrity appears maintained, though bone scan and dedicated radiographic evaluation would provide complementary information if there is specific clinical concern at a particular site).

---

IMPRESSION:

1. HEPATIC METASTASES — PROGRESSING: The two previously confirmed hepatic metastases in the right lobe (segments VI-VII and VI) have grown compared to the prior CT/MRI, now measuring 1.6 cm and 1.4 cm respectively (from 1.2 cm and 1.0 cm). A previously identified 6 mm segment V lesion has grown to 7 mm, consistent with progression. An additional new 5 mm hypodense focus in segment IV is suspicious for a fourth hepatic metastatic deposit. Overall, the hepatic disease burden has increased during the 2-cycle course of carboplatin and cabazitaxel.

2. PULMONARY NODULES — GROWING, CONSISTENT WITH METASTATIC DISEASE: The right lower lobe pulmonary nodules have grown since the prior examination, with the dominant nodule now measuring 12 mm (from 5 mm at initial identification). A third nodule in the right lower lobe appears new. The pattern of growth in multiple right lower lobe nodules in the setting of advanced mCRPC with known visceral spread is highly concerning for pulmonary metastatic disease.

3. RETROPERITONEAL LYMPHADENOPATHY — PROGRESSING: Right para-aortic (1.7 cm) and aortocaval (1.3 cm) nodal disease has grown. Emerging left para-aortic nodal disease.

4. PELVIC LYMPHADENOPATHY — PROGRESSING: The right obturator node has grown to 2.7 cm (from 2.4 cm). Right external iliac nodal disease is stable to mildly increased.

5. EXTENSIVE OSSEOUS METASTATIC DISEASE: Not formally measurable on this CT modality; please correlate with recent bone scintigraphy. No acute pathologic fractures identified. Status post right femoral neck radiation — no acute cortical disruption.

6. MILD SPLENOMEGALY: Likely reactive in the setting of chronic anemia and systemic disease burden. No focal splenic lesion.

7. CHRONIC RENAL PARENCHYMAL CHANGES: Consistent with known CKD history. No acute obstructive uropathy.

Overall, this examination demonstrates clear disease progression in the liver, lungs, and regional lymph nodes despite 2 cycles of systemic chemotherapy with carboplatin and cabazitaxel, indicating a chemotherapy-refractory phenotype. The hepatic and pulmonary metastatic burden has increased, and retroperitoneal and pelvic nodal disease continues to grow.

Radiologist: [Attending Radiologist, Abdominal Imaging / Oncologic Imaging Division]
Dictated and electronically verified.

[Electronically signed]
